segments.s696-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. آن نامه is a demonstrative + noun 'that letter' (object/topic). نزدیک here qualifies the event as 'near/close' and with رسید (past 'reached/arrived') the whole segment reads 'when that letter reached Khosrow' — خسرو is the recipient/name and رسید is the simple past verb 'reached/arrived'.
segments.s696-g2: زپیوستن is the fused poetic form of ز + پیوستن and functions nominally here ('of the joining' / 'about joining'). آگاهی نو is the noun phrase 'new information' (آگاهی = information, نو = new). رسید is again the past verb 'arrived'; together the clause means 'new information about the joining arrived.'
segments.s696-g3: به ایرانیان is the indirect object 'to/for the Iranians' (به + ایرانیان). گفت here is used as the reporting verb 'said/told' (verb sense), introducing the reported content that follows; کامروز is an adverbial 'today/this very day'. مهر in this context is a celestial/deity name (the classical Mithra/astral influence) rather than plain 'love'.
segments.s696-g4: دگرگونه گردد is a verb phrase 'becomes/becomes different' (گردد = become; دگرگونه = different). همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle placed before the verb (here giving an archaic/fixed poetic emphasis, roughly 'indeed/so it will be'). برسپهر is a prepositional/locative phrase 'over/upon the sky/heavens' modifying the verb phrase.
segments.s696-g5: زقیصر is the fused prepositional/name form 'from Caesar' (literally 'of Caesar/from Caesar'); یک نامه آمد is 'one/ a single letter came' (آمد = came/arrived). بلند qualifies the letter as 'lofty/weighty' (an elevated/style or length, a poetic adjective after the noun).
segments.s696-g6: سخن گفتنش is a nominalized genitive construction: سخن ('the speech/content') + گفتنش ('its saying' = the saying of it), so 'the content of what it said.' سر به سر is an idiomatic adverbial ('from head to head' = 'completely/throughout'). سودمند is the predicate adjective 'useful/profitable', so the clause says 'everything it said was wholly useful.'
segments.s696-g7: همی before the verb gives a continuous/habitual sense ('kept' / 'continually'). راه جوید is the verbal phrase 'seeks/searches for a way' (جوید is the archaic-present form of 'to seek'). که introduces the subordinate clause explaining purpose or content: 'that ... دیرینه کین' = 'the long-standing vengeance.'
segments.s696-g8: ببرد is the present/subjunctive-type form of بردن 'to take/carry' expressing the intended action after که ('so that he might carry/take'). ز روم و ز ایران‌زمین are instrumental/source phrases 'from Rome and from the land of Iran' (repeated z is poetic), with روم and ایران‌زمین naming the two realms involved.
segments.s696-g33: هران کس که is a free relative meaning “whoever/any person who.” The verb تواند follows at the end of the clause as in older Persian: تواند = “can/is able” (a modal/presential form). Word order is noun (کس) + relative که + locative phrase در بارگاه + verb تواند.
segments.s696-g34: This is a parallel continuation: ازایران (adverbial/of/from Iran) + و (and) + اندر پناه (in/within protection) + تواند (can/be able). The second تواند repeats the modal to complete the parallel notion “and can be under protection.” اندر is the locative preposition and پناه is the noun “refuge/protection.”
segments.s696-g35: چوگستهم here reads as a temporal/contrastive clause head (چو) attached to گستهم: in older usage چو + verb/phrase = “when/when I became.” The following و شاپور و چون اندیان is a coordination listing names/instances (and Shapur and such as the Andians). Word order is archaic but parallels modern Persian coordination.
segments.s696-g36: چو at the start of this clause functions as ‘when/as’ introducing the event. خراد here functions as a proper/name or person (contextually a rider), بر زین = “upon the saddle,” and زتخم کیان = a genitival/agentive phrase meaning “descended from the seed/of the stock of the Kians.” The clause thus says that Khurad (or ‘wisdom’ figuratively) was on the saddle, born of the Kian line. Note the archaic postpositioning and compact nominal phrases.
segments.s696-g37: چو introduces a temporal clause: 'when'. لشکر فرستی is the verb phrase 'you sent/dispatch an army' (فرستی is an older verbal form meaning 'sent/send'), and بدیشان سپار is an instructive/imperative phrase 'entrust (it) to them' (سپار as a verb meaning 'entrust/deliver' with بدیشان = 'to them'). The object of سپار appears in the next segment.
segments.s696-g38: This nominal group is the direct object: خرد یافته = 'acquired/wise' (خرد here as 'wisdom' adjectivally, یافته 'having been found/possessed'), دختر = 'maiden/daughter', نامدار = 'renowned/famed'. Together they form 'a wisdom‑possessing, renowned maiden' which is what is to be entrusted to the army.
segments.s696-g39: بخویشی is an adverbial/ablative phrase meaning 'by/for kinship' or 'as kin'; چنانم = 'be such to me' / 'treat me so' (چنان + enclitic -م), کنون = 'now', باتو = 'with you', من is the nominative 'I' added for emphasis. The clause is a request/statement asking to be treated now as a kinsman and asserting the speaker's closeness.
segments.s696-g40: چو here functions as 'as/like' introducing a comparison: از پیش = 'formerly/earlier' (از + پیش), بود = past copula 'was', آن بزرگ انجمن = 'that great assembly/council.' The clause means 'as that great council was formerly' or 'as I was formerly in that great assembly.'
segments.s696-g25: هران is an archaic/poetic indefinite demonstrative meaning 'those/any (such)'; it precedes شارستانی, here a noun meaning an urban settlement ('town'). کزان is a relative/prepositional compound 'from which' attaching the following noun مرز ('border, frontier') into a relative clause; بود is the copula in past (here simple past/epic past) agreeing with the implied subject 'those towns' — together: 'those towns which were on the frontier.'
segments.s696-g26: اگر چند functions concessively here (classical usage = 'اگرچند' = 'although / even if'); چند works as a mitigator 'somewhat / a little' or 'though a few'; بیکار describes the town as 'idle / inactive'; و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'; بی‌ارز means 'worthless / of little value'; بود is again the past copula, so the whole phrase reads 'even if it was somewhat idle and of little worth.'
segments.s696-g27: بقیصر is the prepositional phrase 'to/for the Caesar' placed before the verb (typical Persian order permits such preposed indirect objects); سپارد is the finite verb 'entrusted/handed over' (past); همه is the object 'all' and بیک ('bey, chieftain') names what was entrusted — together: 'he entrusted all the beys to Caesar.' Note the compact epic word order where indirect object + verb + object is common.
segments.s696-g28: ازین پس is a temporal phrase 'from this time on / thereafter' (از + این in older spelling); نوشته فرستیم is a first-person-plural verbal phrase 'we sent a written message' (نوشته = 'a written thing / letter', فرستیم = 'we sent'); و is 'and' and چک is a short noun attached after the conjunction — its exact referent is ambiguous/poetic here (the lexeme in this list suggests 'drop' but contextually it may mean a small written note or seal); overall: 'thereafter we sent a written missive and ...' (an appended item).
segments.s696-g29: همان نیز is an emphatic phrase 'that same (one) also / she likewise'; دختر is the noun 'daughter' (subject); کزان is the relative 'from whom / of whom' linking to the next word indicating origin; مادرست here functions as a predicate describing the woman as 'a mother' or 'of the mother' in the relative construction (classical compact syntax often fuses noun+copula). The clause overall names her as 'the same (one) also, the daughter of that mother.'
segments.s696-g30: که introduces a relative clause modifying 'مادر' ('who'); پاکست is a predicate 'is pure / chaste' with the older enclitic -ست for 'is'; وپیوسته is 'and continually/ever' (و+پیوسته written together in the text), and قیصرست is a predicate 'is (of) Caesar / belongs to the Caesar' (again -ست copula). So the relative clause says 'who is pure and continually (in the service/possession) of Caesar.'
segments.s696-g31: بهمداستان is a fused adverbial 'in one accord / in agreement' (bih‑madastān), placed before the verb for emphasis; پدرخواستیم is a fused verb phrase 'we asked the father' (پدر + خواستیم, first-person plural past) — together: 'we, in agreement, asked the father.'
segments.s696-g32: بدین here is instrumental/causal 'by this / with this' (ب + دین functioning as the fixed particle 'بدین'); خواستن is used in its nominal/verbal sense 'the asking / request' and دل is the object 'heart'; بیاراستیم is a past first-person-plural verb 'we adorned / made ready / won' (classical compound verb meaning 'to arrange/adorn' or figuratively 'to win over'). The clause thus reads 'by this request we won/adorned the heart.' Note: بدین خواستن is a compact phrase meaning 'by this asking/request.'
segments.s696-g9: چنین (thus) is an adverb placing the following statement as the reported result; یافت is the past tense 3rd-person singular of یاب/یافت, ‘found/received’; پاسخ زایرانیان is a genitive phrase ‘the answer of the pilgrims’ (پاسخ as the noun, زایرانیان marking whose answer). The verb یافت carries the agent from the wider context (implicit ‘he’).
segments.s696-g10: که introduces reported content (‘that’). هرگز نه is the negator ‘never/by no means’ (هرگز with the particle نه for emphasis). برخاست is past 3rd-singular of برخاستن here meaning ‘to be removed / to depart’ (i.e. to cease to exist). کین ‘grudge/vengeance’ is the noun subject of برخاست (what would not depart), and ازمیان (از میان) indicates removal ‘from the midst’ (here ‘be removed’—i.e. ‘removed away’).
segments.s696-g11: چواین is a fused adverb/conjunction meaning ‘when/if this’; راست is an adjective ‘right/true/correct’; گردد is the 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive of گردیدن ‘to become’ (here ‘becomes/is set right’); بهنگام تو ‘in your time’ (بهنگام = at the appointed/as appropriate time, with تو specifying whose time).
segments.s696-g12: نویسند is 3rd-person plural present/optative (classical/Epic Persian form) of نوشتن and functions here as ‘they will write / they shall write’. برتاجها is a prepositional/locative compound (بر + تاجها) meaning ‘on the crowns’ (برتاجها as the surface/objects on which writing is done). نام تو ‘your name’ is the direct object, with تو as possessive. The clause is a result/temporal clause linked to the previous ‘when’.
segments.s696-g13: چوایشان is a fused/contracted sequence meaning ‘when (they/that) they…’ (چو = when; ایشان = they/them) and sets a temporal clause. بران گونه is a fixed phrase ‘of that kind/that sort’ (بران = بر + آن; گونه = manner/type). دیدند is past 3rd-person plural ‘they saw’; رای (placed at the end) is the noun ‘counsel/opinion’ — together: ‘when they saw that their counsel was of that sort.’
segments.s696-g14: بپردخت is a past 3rd-person singular form of پرداختن/پرداخت meaning ‘he paid/gave/handed over’ (here causative/transfer sense: ‘bestowed/handed over’). خسرو (postverbal) is the agent/name (Khosrow). زبیگانه جای is a beneficiary/recipient phrase: ز (from/unto/by) + بیگانه (a foreigner) + جای (place/position/seat) — together indicating that the place/office was given to (or placed in the hands of) a foreigner.
segments.s696-g15: دوات و قلم ‘an inkstand and a pen’ are coordinated objects (دوات = inkpot/kit; قلم = pen). خواست is the past 3rd-person singular of خواستن ‘wanted/asked for/requested’ (here reporting that he requested these items). وچینی حریر joins on (و + چینی = Chinese/porcelain; حریر = silk), so the full list is ‘inkstand and pen and Chinese silk.’
segments.s696-g16: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb meaning ‘he commanded/ordered’ (literally ‘he said/caused’). تا here has a purposive/result sense ‘so that/whereupon’; پیش او ‘before him / in his presence’; شد is past 3rd-singular of شدن ‘became/happened’ (here auxiliary/result: ‘was brought’/‘came’); دبیر is the noun ‘scribe/secretary’ — together: ‘he ordered so that a scribe was brought before him.’
segments.s696-g49: ز قیصر پذیرفتم آن دخترش is a compact clause meaning “I received/took that daughter from the Caesar.” ز before قیصر is a poetic preposition meaning “from.” پذیرفتم is the past‑tense 1st‑person singular verb “I accepted/received/took,” and آن دخترش follows as the direct object (“that daughter of his/her”), with Persian word order Verb + Object here (pozditional ‘from’ phrase preceding the verb).
segments.s696-g50: که از دختران باشد او افسرش is a relative/clause of purpose/explanation introduced by که (“that/so that”). از دختران is a genitive/partitive phrase “from/among the maidens.” باشد is the present/subjunctive form of بودن used here to express a potential or wished state (“may be / might be / be”), and او افسرش places the pronoun and the possessed noun together: literally “one (she) his‑officer,” i.e. “one of the maidens might be his officer.” The clause therefore gives the intended role or status of that taken daughter.
segments.s696-g51: ازین بر نگردم که گفتم یکی: ازین بر نگردم is an idiomatic negated present/futureish phrase meaning “I will not depart/turn away from this.” ازین = از + این (“from this”), بر نگردم is negative present/subjunctive/optative sense “I do not/ will not turn back.” که گفتم یکی resumes the reported comment: که introduces the content (“that”), گفتم here stands for “I said” and یکی refers to “one (example/instance).” Word order is subject/verb omitted + object; the clause is a commentary on the speaker’s refusal to abandon what was said.
segments.s696-g52: ز کردار بسیار تا اندکی is an elaboration of that ‘one’: ز کردار بسیار = “one from many deeds” (ز = “from,” کردار = “deeds/acts,” بسیار = “many”). تا اندکی here narrows or limits the mention — تا + اندکی expresses a small extent/briefness (“to a small degree / briefly”). The whole phrase functions as an appositive explaining which “one” was spoken of.
segments.s696-g53: تو چیزی که گفتی درنگی مساز is an admonition: تو چیزی که گفتی = “about what you said” (تو = you, چیزی که گفتی = “the thing/that which you said”). درنگی is a noun/infinitive meaning “a pause/hesitation,” and مساز is the negative imperative of ساختن/ساختن‑type verb here meaning “do not make” (i.e., “do not hesitate”). The clause orders the addressee not to delay over their previously uttered words.
segments.s696-g54: که بودن درین شارستان شد دراز gives the reason: که = “for/that,” بودن = the infinitive/nominal form “to be/staying,” درین = در + این (“in this”), شارستان = “city/town,” شد دراز = “has become long.” Together: “for staying in this city has become long,” explaining why one must not delay.
segments.s696-g55: چو کرد این سخن‌ها برین گونه یاد is a temporal clause: چو کرد = “when he made/did,” این سخن‌ها = “these words,” and برین گونه یاد = “as a remembrance in this manner” (برین = بر + این, گونه = manner/kind, یاد = remembrance/record). The clause sets the occasion: when he composed these words into such a memorial.
segments.s696-g56: نوشته بخورشید خراد داد is the main action: نوشته = “the writing/text” (noun), [بخورشید] is here a proper name (recipient) and خراد is the giver’s name (proper name) followed by داد = past tense “gave.” The verb داد governs the object نوشته and names the agent and recipient: “Kharad gave the writing to Bokhurshid.”
segments.s696-g17: یکی (a certain) begins the noun phrase 'a certain letter'; نامه is the direct object 'letter'; بنوشت is the past verb 'wrote' (classical past form); بر پهلوی is a prepositional phrase where بر + پهلوی names the medium/language/script ('in Pahlavi'). Word order is Subject-(adverbial)-Object-Verb typical of older Persian, here reduced to 'one wrote a letter ...'.
segments.s696-g18: برآیین شاهان خط خسروی is a descriptive noun phrase modifying how the letter was written: برآیین ('in the manner/custom of'), شاهان (genitive plural 'of kings'), خط ('hand/script/handwriting'), خسروی ('royal/regal' as adjectival qualifier). The phrase literally: 'in the manner of kings, (in) the royal script.'
segments.s696-g19: که is the relative/complementizer 'which/that'; پذرفت is the simple past 'accepted/took'; خسرو is the subject 'Khosrow'; زیزدان پاک appears as the direct object phrase, with پاک 'pure' qualifying زیزدان. The clause reads 'which Khosrow accepted, the pure زیزدان.' (Note: زیزدان is a literary/rare noun here; see senses mapping.)
segments.s696-g20: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; گردنده خورشید is a genitive phrase 'the revolving/turning sun' (گردنده = 'turning, revolving' modifying خورشید 'sun'); تا introduces the endpoint 'to/until' and governs تیره خاک 'dark earth/dust' as the limit. Together: 'from the revolving sun to the dark earth.'
segments.s696-g21: که 'that/for' + تا 'as long as/until' introduces a temporal clause; او بود شاه is 'he was king' with بود the past copula; در پیشگاه ('in the presence/at court') is a locative adjunct specifying where he was king. Word order places the predicate 'بود شاه' after the subject 'او' (typical classical word order).
segments.s696-g22: ورا ('to him') is the indirect pronoun indicating possession; باشد is a subjunctive/present-form of 'to be' here used optatively/relative ('would be/belong'); ایران و گنج و سپاه lists the things that 'would be his' — 'Iran, and (its) treasure, and (its) army.' The predicate follows the possessor structure: 'to him would be Iran, treasure and army.'
segments.s696-g23: نخواهد is a negative modal/auxiliary meaning 'would not/will not/would not wish' (here modal past/conditional sense); ز is the preposition 'from/on account of'; دارندگان 'the holders/possessors' is a plural noun modifying the following noun; باژ 'cry/noise' qualifies what the holders have (their outcry); روم is the place/name 'Rome/Byzantium' governed by دارندگان. Together: 'would not (act) because of the holders of Rome's outcry.'
segments.s696-g24: نه marks the continuation of the negative ('nor'); لشکر فرستد is the verbal phrase 'send an army' with فرستد in the subjunctive/infinitive‑like form used after the negative auxiliary; بران is a demonstrative/adverb meaning 'to that/thereupon'; مرز وبوم is a fixed pair 'border and land/homeland' marking the destination. So: 'nor send an army to that border and land.'
segments.s696-g41: This is a nominal fronting that names the first pair of legendary kings. نخسـتین is an ordinal modifier placed before the proper name; با links the two names and functions like “and/with” in coordination rather than a literal instrumental.
segments.s696-g42: A short clause with the relative-like k- (کزو) 'from whom' or 'from which' + past copula بود 'was', followed by the theme گیتی and the phrase بیم و امید (here written together as ببیم وامید). Word order is verb-final/nominal: 'from whom was the world [fear and hope]'. بیم و امید is a binomial meaning 'fear and hope'.
segments.s696-g43: دگر هرچ is a verbless nominal phrase meaning 'the rest / whatever (they) are'; هستند is the copula 'are' agreeing with a plural understood subject; ایرج نژاد is the predicate nominal meaning 'of Irāj's stock/lineage'.
segments.s696-g44: Relative clause introduced by که 'who/whom/that'. آیین و فر are the two direct objects/terms ('custom/rite and royal glory'); the clause ends with فریدون نهاد, verb-final: 'Fereydūn nهاد (placed/gave)'. نهاد is the simple past of نهادن 'to place/establish/give'.
segments.s696-g45: بدین here is a compound prepositional/demonstrative 'by/with/through these' (classical style), همنشان 'companions / fellow‑figures' names the group, and تا marks an endpoint 'as far as / down to' followed by the name قباد بزرگ 'Qobād the Great'.
segments.s696-g46: A relative clause introduced by که 'who/whose/that'. از داد او reads 'from/of his justice' (داد used as 'justice'), خویش ش is a possessive cluster 'his own' (خوی + ش), and the remaining token بدمیش (here written together) is an irregular/poetic form that does not match the supplied sense list; وگرگ is 'and wolf' (و + گرگ) appended. The clause structure is verb-final/elliptical and somewhat compressed as is typical in epic diction.
segments.s696-g47: همه کینه برداشتیم is a past-tense verbal clause: همه 'all', کینه 'rancor/hatred', برداشتیم 'we removed/took away' (past 1st pl.). از میان 'from between' is a prepositional complement showing the domain from which the rancor was removed.
segments.s696-g48: یکی گشت is a short past clause meaning 'became one' or 'became united' (یکی = one/united, گشت = became). رومی و ایرانیان names the two groups that are united: 'the Romans and the Iranians.' Word order is subject-predicate with the predicate first (idiomatic poetic inversion).
segments.s696-g65: به نزدیک قیصر is a prepositional phrase meaning 'toward/near the Caesar' (به + نزدیک + noun). انجمن is the subject 'the assembly' and شدند is the past plural verb 'they came/approached' placed after the phrase, a common poetic word order (prepositional phrase + verb + subject).
segments.s696-g66: بپرسید is third‑person past 'he asked' (here the subject is the Caesar implied from the previous clause). زیشان is the contracted prepositional object 'from them / of them'. همه تن is a noun phrase 'the whole body' (همه modifying تن) and بتن qualifies it (here 'of stone/solid'), forming the content of the question he put to them. Word order is verb → oblique phrase → question fragment.
segments.s696-g67: که introduces the indirect question/clause. اکنون = 'now'. مر is an archaic dative/benefactive ('to/for me/us') used here with a first‑person plural verb construction; این را is the object 'this' with the object marker را. چه درمان کنیم is an interrogative phrase 'what remedy/what shall (we) do' with کنیم as the first‑person plural subjunctive/optative meaning 'shall we do'.
segments.s696-g68: ابا is a verb meaning 'to be reluctant / to refuse' used here as a rhetorical question ('shall we refuse...?'). شاه ایران is the person referenced ('the King of Iran'). چه پیمان کنیم is a second interrogative clause 'what pact/treaty shall we make', again with کنیم as 1st plural subjunctive; the two questions are linked without an explicit conjunction.
segments.s696-g69: بدین نامه is an instrumental/causal phrase 'by this letter' (بدین = 'by this'), ما is the subject 'we', بی‌بهانه is a predicate adjective meaning 'without blame / blameless / without excuse', and شدیم is the past plural 'we became' — together: 'by this letter we became blameless.' Note the archaic split orthography (ب + دین) in the corpus.
segments.s696-g70: همی is an adverbial particle here meaning roughly 'thus/so'; روم و ایران is a coordinate noun phrase 'Rome and Iran'; یگانه is the predicate adjective 'one/united'; شدیم is again past plural 'we became' — 'thus Rome and Iran became one.' The verb appears at the end, as typical in the poetic narrative.
segments.s696-g71: بزرگان فرزانه is a noun phrase 'the great/elder wise ones' with the adjective فرزانه following the noun (common in classical Persian). برخاستند is past plural 'they rose/stood up', agreeing with the plural noun.
segments.s696-g72: زبان را uses زبان 'speech/tongue' with the definite/object marker را to mark the thing being prepared. به پاسخ is a purpose/benefactive phrase 'for an answer', and بیاراستند (بی- + آراستن) is past plural 'they adorned/arranged' — together: 'they arranged/adorned their speech for a reply.' Word order places the object before the final verb.
segments.s696-g73: که here introduces the clause and functions like 'for'/'that' (a discourse connective). ما کهترانیم is a verbless equational clause: ما (we) + کهترانیم (we-are-lesser) — کهترانیم is a copular/adjectival predicate in present tense. و links the coordinate clause قیصر تویی, where تویی is the 2nd-person singular present copula 'thou art' following the predicate noun (قيصر) — word order is Predicate + Copula (common in older/poetic Persian).
segments.s696-g74: جهاندار با تخت و افسر تویی is another equational clause: جهاندار (world-ruler/sovereign) is the predicate, followed by the possessive/associated instrumental phrase با تخت و افسر ('with throne and helmet/crown'), and finalized by the copula تویی ('thou art'). The preposition با introduces what accompanies or adorns the sovereign; افسر here means a head-ornament/helmet used poetically as 'crown'.
segments.s696-g75: نگه کن is an imperative ('look!' or 'see!') formed by noun/verb pair نگه + کن (do/look). کنون is an adverb 'now' modifying the following predication. رای و فرمان تو راست is a verbless predication: رای و فرمان (your counsel and command) + تو (possessive 'your') with the predicate راست ('right/true'); the copula 'is/are' is omitted, typical in Persian equational clauses.
segments.s696-g76: ز ما‌گر بخواهی is a conditional clause with inverted older ordering: ز ما (from us) + گر (if) + بخواهی (you-wish/you-request, present-subjunctive/indicative used in a conditional). The main clause 'تن و جان تو راست' is again verbless: 'تن و جان' (body and life) + تو (your) + راست (are right/are at your disposal) — i.e. 'body and soul are yours' if you ask.
segments.s696-g77: چو بشنید is a temporal subordinate clause: چو ('when') + بشنید (past tense 'he heard'). The main clause places the subject after the subordinate: قیصر گرفت آفرین — گرفت ('took'/'gave') is a past-tense verb used idiomatically with آفرین ('bravo/acclamation') to mean 'uttered praise'. Word order (verb then subject) reflects older/poetic flexibility.
segments.s696-g78: بدان نامداران با رای و دین: بدان is a dative/locative phrase ('to those' / 'to that/thereupon') formed by the prefix b- + an older element; نامداران is the plural noun 'renowned ones' (recipients of the praise). با رای و دین ('with counsel and religion/piety') is an instrumental/explanatory phrase giving the grounds of the praise. The whole phrase functions as the object/target and circumstance of the verb آفرین (he praised them for their counsel and piety).
segments.s696-g79: همی‌بود is an imperfect/continuous past ('it continued' / 'was continuing'). تاشمع (تا + شمع) is a temporal 'until the candle...' (a poetic image for the celestial lamp), گردان is an adjective 'revolving' modifying شمع, and سپهر means 'sky' or 'heaven' — together 'until the sky's revolving candle' marks the temporal limit of the action.
segments.s696-g80: دگرگونه ترشد is a verbal phrase meaning 'became different/changed' — دگرگونه (different) + ترشد (became/grew) with ترشد here used as a past/resultative verb. به آیین و چهر is a prepositional phrase ('in manner/custom and in visage/appearance') specifying the domain of the change; آیین = manner/custom, چهر = face/appearance.
segments.s696-g57: This whole chunk is a clipped simile + motion clause: 'سپهبد' is the subject, then 'چو باد' (= 'as/like the wind') is a simile modifying the manner of coming, and 'اندر آمد زجای' is the verbal phrase 'entered/came in from (his) place/post'. Word order is subject → manner phrase → verb + source; 'اندر آمد' is simple past 'came in'.
segments.s696-g58: This clause expresses the following action: 'اندر آورد پای' is an idiom meaning 'set foot/advanced'. 'باسپ کمیت' appears before the verb and supplies the agent/means (likely 'with Komit’s forces' = با سپِ کمیت), but the provided lexical senses do not include that precise reading, so the two first words are probably a compressed/archaic phrase (با سپ + a proper name). 'آورد' is the past 'brought/put' used here in the idiomatic construction with 'پای' (= 'foot' → 'set foot/advance').
segments.s696-g59: 'همی‌تاخت' is a past continuous/habitual poetic form (prefix همی + past stem) meaning 'was charging/kept charging'. 'تا' here marks the endpoint 'up to/until', and 'پیش قیصر' means 'before/in the presence of the Caesar'. 'چوباد' is a contracted poetic form of 'چو باد' (= 'like the wind') functioning as an adverbial manner phrase attached to the whole action.
segments.s696-g60: This is an object + indirect object + verb cluster. 'سخنهای خسرو' = 'the utterances/words of Khosrow' (possessive noun phrase). 'بدو' = 'to him' (indirect object). 'کرد یاد' literally 'made a remembrance' — an idiomatic verbal phrase meaning 'recounted/recited/reminded him of'. The noun 'یاد' is used with 'کرد' to form the action 'to remind/recite'.
segments.s696-g61: 'چو' introduces a temporal clause 'when'. 'قیصر' is the subject; 'ازان نامه' = 'of that letter/from that letter' (the object/source), and 'بگسست بند' is the verb + object: 'بگسست' = past 'broke (open)', 'بند' here is 'bond/band/fastening' (i.e., the letter’s seal or binding). The order is subject → object → verb phrase, typical of classical Persian narrative.
segments.s696-g62: 'بدید' is simple past 'he saw'. The noun phrase 'آن سخنهای شاه بلند' follows: 'آن' = 'those/that', 'سخنهای شاه' = 'the king’s words', and 'بلند' is a postpositive adjective meaning 'lofty' (high/eloquent). Together: 'he saw those lofty words of the king.'
segments.s696-g63: 'بفرمود تا' is a formulaic construction 'he commanded that...' introducing a subjunctive/purpose clause. 'هر که دانا' = 'whoever is learned/wise'. The verb 'بدند' here functions as a subjunctive/optative plural 'should be / be brought' (classical usage where -ند marks the subjunctive in such constructions).
segments.s696-g64: 'به گفتارها' expresses purpose/role 'for the speeches / to speak'. 'بر توانا' = 'before the powerful' (بر + adjective/noun = 'in front of / toward the powerful'). The final 'بدند' repeats the subjunctive sense ('should be brought/presented'). Overall: '...be brought forward to speak before the powerful.'

sentences.s696-s1: When that letter reached Khosrow, fresh news arrived of the joining.
sentences.s696-s2: He told the Iranians, 'Today Mehr will change its aspect over the sky.'
sentences.s696-s3: A single lofty letter came from Caesar; everything it said was useful.
sentences.s696-s4: He kept seeking a way to carry off the long-standing vengeance from Rome and from the land of Iran.
sentences.s696-s17: Whoever can be in the court and be under the protection of Iran.
sentences.s696-s18: As I became (or when I was) Gostaham, and Shapur, and the like of the Andians; and when Khurad mounted the saddle, descended from the seed of the Kians.
sentences.s696-s19: When you send an army, entrust to them a wise, accomplished maiden famed by name.
sentences.s696-s20: Treat me kindly as kin now, I am with you, as before that great assembly was.
sentences.s696-s13: Any town that lay on the frontier, even if it was somewhat idle and of little value.
sentences.s696-s14: He entrusted all the beys to the Caesar; thereafter we sent a written message (and a seal/dispatch).
sentences.s696-s15: She, too, was the daughter of that mother — who was pure and continually (faithful) to the Caesar.
sentences.s696-s16: In agreement we asked the father; by this request we won/adorned the heart.
sentences.s696-s5: Thus he received the pilgrims' reply: that the grudge would never be removed.
sentences.s696-s6: When this is made right, in your time they will inscribe your name on the crowns.
sentences.s696-s7: When they saw that their counsel was of that sort, Khosrow handed the position over to a foreigner.
sentences.s696-s8: He asked for an inkstand and a pen and Chinese silk, and ordered that a scribe be brought before him.
sentences.s696-s25: I received that daughter from the Caesar, so that one of the maidens might be his officer.
sentences.s696-s26: I will not turn away from this; I have spoken (of) one — one example from many deeds, a little.
sentences.s696-s27: Do not delay about what you said, for staying in this city has become long.
sentences.s696-s28: When he put these words down as a remembrance in this manner, Khurad gave the writing to Bokhurshid.
sentences.s696-s9: He wrote a letter in Pahlavi, in the royal hand according to the manner of kings.
sentences.s696-s10: which Khosrow accepted — the pure zizdān — from the revolving sun to the dark earth.
sentences.s696-s11: For as long as he was king in the court, Iran and its treasure and army would belong to him.
sentences.s696-s12: He would not, on account of the holders of Rome's outcry, send an army to that borderland.
sentences.s696-s21: First Kayūmart and Jamshīd — from them the world was full of fear and hope.
sentences.s696-s22: The others — whatever they are — belong to the lineage of Irāj, whom Fereydūn endowed with ritual and royal glory.
sentences.s696-s23: Thus in this cohort down to Qobād the Great — whose justice (or by whose justice) his own… (bدمیش) and the wolf.
sentences.s696-s24: We removed all rancor from between them; the Romans and the Iranians became one.
sentences.s696-s33: The assembly drew near to Caesar; he asked them, "Is his whole body of stone?"
sentences.s696-s34: Now what shall we do about this? Shall we refuse the King of Iran, and what treaty shall we make?
sentences.s696-s35: By this letter we were acquitted/blameless; thus Rome and Iran became one.
sentences.s696-s36: The wise elders rose and groomed (adorned) their speech into an answer.
sentences.s696-s37: For we are the lesser ones and you are Caesar; you are the sovereign of the world, with throne and helmet (crown).
sentences.s696-s38: Look — now your counsel and command are right; if you ask of us, body and life are yours.
sentences.s696-s39: When the Caesar heard, he gave an acclamation; he praised those renowned men for their counsel and piety.
sentences.s696-s40: It continued until the sky's revolving candle; then it changed in manner and appearance.
sentences.s696-s29: The spahbed came in like the wind from his post, and with Komit’s troops he advanced.
sentences.s696-s30: He charged like the wind up to the presence of the Caesar, and he recited Khosrow’s words to him.
sentences.s696-s31: When the Caesar broke open that letter’s fastening, he saw those lofty words of the king.
sentences.s696-s32: He ordered that whoever was learned be brought forward to speak before the powerful.
